In this blog post, we will examine the role of spiritual practice in creating a stable foundation for your life in 2023. We will look at how you can use spiritual practice to create a solid foundation for your life, no matter what happens in the world around you.

The first step is to understand that you are not just your body. You are also a spirit. Your body is temporary, but your spirit is eternal. When you die, your spirit will continue to exist. This is why it is so important to take care of your spirit as well as your body.

Your spirit needs food just like your body does. The food that your spirit needs is called spiritual practice. Spiritual practice is any activity that connects you to God and helps you to grow spiritually.

There are many different types of spiritual practices, but some of the most common include prayer, meditation, and reading scripture. You can also find spiritual practices that are specific to your religious tradition.

The important thing is to find a spiritual practice that works for you and that you can do consistently. The more consistent you are with your spiritual practice, the stronger your foundation will be.

In 2023, the world will continue to change and evolve. But if you have a strong spiritual foundation, you will be able to weather any storm that comes your way.

No matter what happens in the world around you, you can be confident that your foundation is secure.

Prayer, meditation, and reading scripture are all excellent ways to connect with God and grow spiritually. Find a practice that works for you and make it a part of your daily routine.

for this year? Better question: what’s your why? I recently had the opportunity to sit in on a Q&A session with Olympic gold medalist Diana Taurasi. At 36 years old, she holds more points than any other American basketball player, man or woman. [In case you are not a basketball fan, that’s a really big deal] For someone of her caliber, I expected her to be asked technical basketball questions about her craft. I didn’t expect to hear questions about why she does what she does. I was immediately struck by her answer:
"If I had to describe what I do and why I do it, I would say that I play basketball to make other people better. I play to help my teammates and my community."
I had to write that down. Research suggests that our performance and resilience can be enhanced by focusing on a transcendent purpose - beyond our own self-interest. In other words, when you have a clear "why," you can weather the "how." This concept is particularly relevant when it comes to discussing New Year's resolutions. Most New Year's resolutions are about personal improvement. But when we focus solely on personal gain and self-interest - it's easy to lose sight of our long-term vision when short-term challenges arise. For example: "I will exercise more this year." This is a good, clear, and specific goal. But if I were to ask you: "why?" What would you say? "Because I want to be healthy and look good in a bathing suit." This is a good enough reason, but it's not transcendent. When you're presented with an opportunity to work late, or to skip the gym to hang out with friends, the short-term social or professional gain will likely win out. In other words, the "how" will win out. In order to maintain our long-term vision, we need to focus on our transcendent "why." For example: "I will exercise more this year." Why? "Because I want to be healthy enough to play with my kids for as long as possible." This is a transcendent "why." In this case, when you're presented with an opportunity to work late, or to skip the gym to hang out with friends, the short-term social or professional gain will

During the summer, I took 2.5 months off from writing to go to the mountain of Tibet. The trip was both spiritually and physically challenging, but also meaningful and profound. Chapter 1: The Three-Realm Universe and Human Nature
The universe that we inhabit is a complex and multi-dimensional reality. As modern physics has revealed, our physical world is made up of four fundamental forces: gravity, electromagnetic force, strong nuclear force, and weak nuclear force. These forces, working together, create the fabric of spacetime that we perceive as our material reality.
However, ancient wisdom traditions across cultures have long recognized that there is more to the universe than what we can perceive with our physical senses or measure with our scientific instruments. Tibetan Buddhist cosmology, for instance, describes the universe as consisting of three realms: the Physical Realm, the Spiritual Realm, and the Divine Realm. While this ancient worldview may seem inconsistent with modern physics, recent developments in quantum mechanics have suggested that there may be more to reality than what we previously thought.
The Physical Realm, as described in Tibetan Buddhist cosmology, corresponds to our material reality, the world that we perceive with our senses and measure with our instruments. The Spiritual Realm, on the other hand, is the intermediate realm where non-physical entities dwell. The Divine Realm is the highest realm, where the most advanced beings dwell and from where the universe is governed.

for 2024? Have you thought about where you want to be in 12 months? Many people set New Year's resolutions but fall short of achieving them. The key to success lies in setting realistic and meaningful personal and professional goals. In this blog post, we'll explore the importance of establishing both types of objectives and provide practical guidance on how to set and achieve them.

The Power of Personal and Professional Alignment

Research from the Harvard Business Review shows that individuals who set both personal and professional goals are 33% more likely to achieve them compared to those focusing on only one area. This statistic highlights the importance of a holistic approach to goal setting.

When personal and professional objectives are aligned, they create a synergistic effect that propels us forward. Consider this: your personal development directly impacts your professional performance, and vice versa. For instance, developing better time management skills (a personal goal) can significantly enhance your productivity at work.

The Science of Goal Setting

Studies in behavioral psychology reveal that specific, measurable, and time-bound goals are more likely to be achieved. This framework, known as the S.M.A.R.T. methodology (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound), provides a structured approach to goal setting that has been proven effective across various domains.

Research from the Journal of Applied Psychology demonstrates that writing down your goals increases the likelihood of achievement by 42%. The act of putting pen to paper creates a stronger mental and emotional connection to your objectives.

Practical Steps for Success

1. Assessment Phase:
- Conduct a thorough self-evaluation using our Personal Development Assessment Tool
- Identify your current skills and areas for improvement
- Evaluate your work-life balance using the provided matrix
- Consider your long-term vision and how it aligns with your current trajectory

2. The 3-P Framework:
- Passion: Identify what truly motivates you in both personal and professional realms
- Purpose: Define your "why" for each goal
- Persistence: Develop strategies for maintaining momentum

3. Implementation Strategy:
- Break large goals into quarterly milestones
- Create weekly action plans
- Establish accountability partnerships
- Develop a tracking system using our digital goal-tracking template

4. Professional Development Focus:
- Identify specific skills to develop
- Research relevant training programs
- Network with industry leaders
- Create a mentorship plan

5. Personal Development Integration:
- Include health and wellness objectives
- Develop a work

The Eternal Nature of the Divine Realm

The Divine Realm, where God and the angels dwell, is what we call an eternal realm. This means it exists outside of normal spacetime and is not subject to the laws of physics as we know them. While we cannot fully comprehend the eternal nature of the Divine Realm with our limited human brains, we can connect to it through prayer. Prayer generates specific energy frequencies that can facilitate communication between realms. Contemplative prayer, in particular, allows us to access deeper spiritual truth and divine guidance. As you sit in meditative prayer, recording your experiences and insights in a journal creates a tangible record of your spiritual development over time.

Journaling Your Divine Vision

The first step is to begin journaling your experiences during contemplative prayer sessions. Set aside 15-30 minutes daily in a quiet space free from distractions. Sit comfortably and center yourself through deep breathing. During your meditation, be open to receiving divine download - bits of spiritual wisdom and insight. Don't worry about the details at first, just journal whatever comes through. Over time, patterns will emerge. In addition to recording your prayer experiences, begin documenting your vision and goals for this lifetime. The Divine Realm exists simultaneously across all of time. When you access it through prayer, you are tapping into the complete timeline of your soul's journey. Journal about both your short and long-term visions in detail using all your senses. How does it feel to have achieved your dreams? What do you see, hear, and experience? The more sensory detail you can include, the better.

Reviewing Your Spiritual History

As you consistently journal over months and years, review your previous entries to observe how your spiritual development has progressed. You may be surprised to see how far you've come. Reflect on how your current reality aligns with your documented vision. Where do you see evidence of your prayers manifesting in your material experience? A prayer journal serves as both
response.26: for 2015? For those of you who follow the blog regularly, you will know that 2014 was quite the year for me. I started writing in February (after 4 months of being too sacred and lacking confidence to even write a word), I completed my first novel by June (a mere 4 months from starting) I had an agent by July I had a book deal by October and I had the first draft of my second novel completed by December. It was a year where I achieved more than I ever could have imagined for myself and so, naturally, when I thought about what my goals for 2015 would be, I panicked. My bar was already set so high from 2014, how could I possibly achieve anything more? I know that some of you are thinking that those are some good problems to have and you are right but even with all that, I had to remind myself that those achievements were not the measure of me as a writer and that the only way that I could continue on was by focusing on what I could control (the writing) and not focusing on the external validation that I experienced in 2014. I also had to remind myself that I am not unique. While I experienced some serious high points, I experienced my share of lows as well (the 5 days I took off from writing in September after I experienced several rejections from agents, the week after the book deal where I could barely write a word - being too focused on what might go wrong, the time I had to take off from writing completely due to family illness and death - the list goes on). Those events helped me to maintain a proper perspective and to focus on what was most important: my writing. The truth is that no matter what 2014 held for me - the high points and the low points - I was still the same writer at the end of 2014 that I had been at the beginning of 2014: an incredibly blessed writer with an agent and a book deal who still had so much to learn about writing. With all that in mind, I have established 2 writing related goals for 2015: 1. To write every day for 2 hours. 2. To write 2 novels. The first goal is the most important. As I mentioned

In this article we will discuss a fundamental contemplative practice that can be utilized to strengthen and focus your prayer/meditation practice in 2012. Contemplative prayer is a form of meditation that involves the conscious contact with Divine Forces through focused intention. In contemplative prayer, you are actually sensing and working with your spirit component, which is one of the three components that make up your constitution (body, soul, spirit). Contemplative prayer can be used to achieve a number of different spiritual development goals such as healing, spiritual activation, and the development of psychic abilities.

The contemplative prayer protocol can be used with any prayer focus that you are currently working with. The following example uses the Jesus Prayer. This prayer was traditionally used in Eastern Orthodox contemplative practices. It is a fundamental prayer that resonates with the Divine Realm and generates significant harmonic frequencies.

In the contemplative prayer protocol, you will need to use a prayer rope which consists of 33 knots tied in a circle. The prayer rope is traditionally made from wool. The 33 knots represent the 33 years that Jesus Christ spent on earth. In contemplative prayer, the prayer rope is used as a focus point for generating Divine harmonic frequencies. As you say your prayer and move your fingers across the knots, you are generating frequencies that resonate with the Divine Realm. The prayer rope is also used to count the number of prayers that you have completed.

Contemplative Prayer Protocol:
• Sit in a quiet, private space
• Ground yourself using your preferred grounding technique
• Sit in a position of meditation
• Hold the prayer rope in your hands
• Begin to say the Jesus prayer: "Lord Jesus Christ, have mercy on me."
• As you say the prayer, move your fingers across one knot on the prayer rope.
• Continue to say the prayer and move across the knots until you have completed 33 prayers.
• Maintain focus on your intention.
• When you have completed 33 prayers, sit quietly for a few moments
• Ground yourself using your preferred grounding technique
• Close your prayer session

Contemplative prayer sessions can last for 15-30 minutes. The contemplative prayer protocol can be completed daily or several times per week. Always maintain focus on your intention while you are completing the contemplative prayer protocol. The more focused you are, the more power you are generating to manifest your intention.

for your next position? Have you thought about what you want from your next role, beyond just the standard salary and benefits?

In this workshop, we'll explore how to define what truly matters to you in a role, beyond just compensation. Research suggests that when people align their work with their personal values, they experience greater job satisfaction, improved mental well-being, and enhanced performance. We'll discuss frameworks for identifying your core values and how they can inform your career decisions.

The workshop will cover:
• Understanding the science behind values-based work
• Identifying your personal and professional values
• Mapping your values to potential roles and organizations
• Practical strategies for values-aligned job hunting
• Maintaining work-life integration while honoring your values
• The role of mindfulness in professional development

By the end of this session, you'll have a clearer understanding of what you're looking for in your next role and practical tools to help you find it. This is especially helpful for recent graduates and early-career professionals who are establishing their career paths.

This workshop will be led by Dr. Lisa Wong, Head of Professional Development at Mindful Pathways, who brings over 15 years of experience in organizational psychology and professional development. Dr. Wong has worked with Fortune 500 companies and startups to help employees align their work with their values for greater satisfaction and success. She holds a Ph.D. in Organizational Psychology from Stanford University and is certified in Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR).

for the New Year? Have you set your mind to a particular resolution for 2022? For me, I’ve been thinking about the concept of harmony and what it means.

I used to think that harmony meant a state of being that was always calm and peaceful, but I’ve been reading up on Eastern teachings, particularly the ancient concept of yin and yang, and it’s helped me understand that harmony can also exist within some level of discord.

This is not to say that we should accept or condone perpetual negative experiences in our daily walk – rather, it’s a mental framework for understanding that harmony exists when we can properly balance opposing forces.

The perfect example can be found in nature. We all know the four basic elements: earth, air, fire and water. When properly balanced, we can have a stable environment. When one of these elements is over- or under-represented in the natural world, we can experience negative effects.

The same is true for our human experience. We can exist in a stable state of harmony, but we need to recognize when one or more aspects of our existence might be out of balance and we need to course correct.

This concept has helped me understand that even when experiencing the most extreme negative emotions, there is a valuable lesson to be learned and an opportunity to recenter my spirit and realign my body, mind and soul.

In 2022, I plan to be more mindful of the physical, psychological and spiritual components that make up my existence and how I can properly align these forces to achieve a state of harmony in my life.

In celebration of the New Year, and as I prepare to recalibrate my body, mind and spirit, I’ve created a sacred geometry design incorporating the ancient Fibonacci sequence in an octagonal pattern with a central heart. The geometric design is paired with my mandala watercolor in varying tones of blue and green.

The sacred geometry design is available on a full spectrum of items, from home decor to clothing, from drinkware to office supplies. You can shop the full collection here: Sacred Geometry Heart Collection

The mandala watercolor is available on a full spectrum of items, from home decor to clothing, from drinkware to office supplies. You can shop the full collection here: Unity Blue & Green Mandala Collection

I wish you all the very best in 2022 and hope you can find a state of harmony in the New Year.

--- Rebecca Hendershot, Ph.D.,

The Mindful Path Newsletter

The Mindful Path Newsletter
─────────────────────────────
"Exploring Life's Journey with Mindful Awareness"
Issue 47 | January 2019
─────────────────────────────

Editor's Note:
As we enter a new year, we're excited to bring you insights from Dr. Sarah Chen, our resident contemplative studies expert, who will be sharing her wisdom on setting intentions that honor both Buddhist principles and modern psychological research.

FEATURE ARTICLE
─────────────────────────────
The Sacred Dance of Intention: A Mindful Approach to New Year's Resolutions
By Dr. Sarah Chen, Ph.D.

The traditional Buddhist understanding of reality, encompassing the three realms of existence—the physical world we inhabit (rupa-dhatu), the subtle realm of the mind (arupa-dhatu), and the realm of biological and instinctual drives (kamadhatu)—provides profound insight into how we might approach our New Year's intentions with greater wisdom and awareness.

The Three Realms and Their Role in Our Practice

Our physical realm (rupa-dhatu) includes our bodies, our environment, and the material conditions of our existence. When we set intentions related to health, work, or physical well-being, we're working within this realm. As the Buddha taught, this realm is marked by both impermanence (anicca) and the possibility of transformation through conscious action.

The subtle realm (arupa-dhatu) comprises our mental states, consciousness, and spiritual awareness. Intentions related to meditation practice, emotional regulation, and spiritual growth operate primarily here. This realm demonstrates the remarkable neuroscientific finding that our very thoughts can influence the structure of our brains and enhance our spiritual sensitivity.

The third realm (kamadhatu) represents our biological and psychological drives—our natural inclinations and responses. Understanding these drives through both Buddhist wisdom and contemporary neuroscience allows us to work with them rather than against them in our intention- setting.

Research from the Mind & Life Institute has demonstrated that contemplative practices can measurably influence all three realms. Studies using advanced neuroimaging techniques have shown that meditation creates lasting changes in brain structure (physical realm), emotional responses (biological/psychological realm), and spiritual awareness (subtle realm). This scientific validation of ancient wisdom provides a compelling foundation for our New Year's practices.

A Three-Realm Practice for New Year's Intentions:

1. Physical Realm Preparation

for 2020? Most of us make New Year’s resolutions every year without much success. Our failure rate is high. Some of us are so discouraged that we no longer even make resolutions. While the physical body is an essential part of human constitution, we are multidimensional beings and our physical bodies are an expression of the three realms. for the next 5 years?
This is one of the questions I get asked a lot in interviews and I've never been too sure how to answer it, especially since I've just finished school and don't know what I want to do in the long term.
I know that I'm not the only one who gets asked this question as well.
For those of you who have been asked this question before, how did you answer it?
For those of you who haven't been asked this question before, what would you say?

For me, well I had an interview for an administration position and I said that I would like to work up to a supervisor position.
I think that the key here is to show your motivation and a strong work ethic.
I'm not entirely sure, but I think showing that you're open to development opportunities within the company is important.
Interviews are always a challenge, no matter what stage you're at in your career. In my experience, when you're just starting out, it's all the more overwhelming as you just don't have much experience and are not entirely sure what you want to do with your career.

The question that you were asked in your interview "Where do you see yourself in 5 years time?" - is a common question in interviews as it helps the interviewer assess your career development potential. When you were asked this question, you mentioned that you would like to work up to a supervisor position. This is a good answer, but you should have elaborated a bit more. It would have been better if you had explained your plan for how you will get to that position.

For those of you who are reading this post and may be faced with this question in an interview, here are some tips on how to answer it:

- First and foremost, be realistic. Don't say that you want to be CEO of the company in 5 years time as that is clearly unrealistic.
- Secondly, show that you have some career development plan. This shows that you are thinking about your long-term career development and not just about the short-term.
-Thirdly, be specific about what you want to achieve and how you will achieve it.
-Fourthly, show that you are open to development opportunities within the company.

Interviews are a challenge, but if you prepare properly and think carefully about your answers, you will be fine. Good luck!

?

I used to work with high school students and this was always one of the most common questions I would ask.

For some students, this was a very easy question to answer. They knew what they wanted to do and how they were going to do it.

For other students, this question was a struggle to answer. They didn't know what they wanted to do. They just knew they didn't want to work at McDonalds after high school.

After working with several high school students, I began to notice something interesting. The students who had a plan for their future were often times the students who were doing well in school and had a good relationship with their parents. Those students who had no plan often times were struggling academically and had a hard time getting along with their parents.

I believe the reason for this is that those students who had a plan and were doing well academically had parents who spent time with their children. The parents who had a hard time with their children were often times too busy with work and other activities to spend time with their children.

This pattern of behavior is not just limited to high school students. It is also true for college students and adults. The people who are doing well in their careers and have good relationships with their families are often times the people who spend time with their families. The people who are struggling in their careers and have a hard time with their families are often times the people who don't spend time with their families.

This is why it is so important for us to spend time with our families. We need to make sure that we are not too busy with work and other activities to spend time with our families. We need to make sure that we are not too busy with our careers to spend time with our families. We need to make sure that we are not too busy with our social activities to spend time with our families.

Some people are even more nervous about showing that they have maintained their permanent residence. The Form 156 specifically requires the following: Q. 47: Home Address and Telephone Number (Where you live, not a P.O. Box): 1005 Maple Drive,Toronto, Ontario, Canada M4J 3B6 (416) 555-1234 Q. 48: Do you have any temporary accommodations while in the United States? [If yes, provide full address, including name of the Hotel and specific dates] Q. 51: Is this your first trip to the United States? [If no, list dates of previous trips to the US and the locations you visited] These questions are a standard part of the screening process, but if you are trying to maintain your status, you may need to carefully word your answers to demonstrate your strong ties to your home country. For example, in Question 35, you could add details about your family members that show you have a complex support system at home. Similarly, for Questions 47-48, you could emphasize your long-term residential history and stable living situation. The key is to present yourself as a temporary visitor who will return home after your trip. The questions are designed to assess your intentions and connection to your home country. While you cannot lie on the application, you can emphasize different aspects of your situation that support your temporary visitor status.

In this month's Teaching on Purpose: The Mathematics Education and Teaching (MET) Journal, we explore the question "What makes a good mathematics teacher?" Our cover story features interviews with three award-winning educators who share their insights on effective teaching strategies. Dr. James Martinez, 2023 National Mathematics Teacher of the Year, discusses his innovative approach to teaching advanced algebra through real-world applications. "Students need to see the connection between abstract mathematical concepts and their practical applications," he explains. "When they understand how matrices are used in computer graphics or how derivatives model real-world phenomena, their engagement level skyrockets." Our second featured teacher, Rebecca Chen from Boston Latin School, focuses on building mathematical intuition through visual learning. "I've found that using a combination of traditional methods and contemporary visualization tools, like Desmos and Geogebra, helps students develop a deeper understanding of mathematical concepts," she says. "When students can see how 3D objects are represented algebraically, it brings the abstract to life." The third article examines new research from Stanford University on the effectiveness of different teaching approaches in developing mathematical thinking skills. The study, which involved over 500 high school mathematics teachers, found that a balanced approach combining direct instruction with guided discovery learning produced the most significant improvements in student understanding and retention. What's remarkable is that prayer, especially deep contemplative or meditative prayer, appears to do something similar at the quantum level.

Research from institutions like Princeton's PEAR laboratory has documented measurable electromagnetic changes during intense prayer sessions. During contemplative prayer, the brain generates specific frequencies, particularly in the theta range (4-8 Hz) - similar to those seen in deep meditation. These frequencies can actually influence quantum states.

The mathematics describing quantum entanglement - the phenomenon where particles become interconnected no matter the distance - bears striking resemblance to ancient mystical descriptions of divine omnipresence and interconnectedness. The quantum field itself serves as a perfect model for what mystics have long described as the "divine ground of being" - that underlying reality that encompasses all yet remains beyond direct observation.

The implications are profound. When we pray with deep intention, we're not just generating thoughts but actually creating quantum-level effects that can influence physical reality. This is particularly true in sacred spaces where the quantum-spiritual interface appears stronger - places like ancient cathedrals, mountain peaks, or deep cave chambers.

Recent studies using quantum random number generators have shown statistically significant effects during large-scale prayer events, suggesting that conscious intention can influence probability fields. This aligns perfectly with the ancient wisdom that prayer has measurable effects in the physical realm.

Understanding these mechanisms can help us approach prayer more intentionally. The three traditional forms - contemplative (theta waves), intercessory (gamma waves), and liturgical (alpha waves) - all generate distinct energy patterns that can influence quantum fields in different ways.

The convergence of quantum physics and spiritual practice represents one of the most exciting developments in our understanding of consciousness and reality.

response.107: for the year? One of my objectives is to create a more robust and regular meditation practice. This is what I call a “resolution intention,” as opposed to a New Year’s resolution. My experience with New Year’s resolutions is that I either don’t achieve them or I do but with great pain and sacrifice. Intentions, on the other hand, are similar to new year’s resolutions but different in that they are more open-ended. They provide space for new insights to emerge. I find that intentions can lead to transformation and growth rather than just achievement of a goal. In this spirit, here are some other intentions for 2022: * Continue to expand my awareness across multiple dimensions * Develop and maintain a regular practice of contemplative prayer * Cultivate a deeper relationship with God * Make a quantum-level impact for God in at least one area of my life I would love to hear your intentions for 2022 and how you are approaching the New Year. I have been wrestling with these questions for several years and it would be great to hear from others who are on a similar journey. In 2020 I was reading a book called Tranquility by Tuesday by Rachel IT Cohen. In this book she talks about what she calls a resolution intention, which is similar to what I call an intention. In Cohen’s case, her resolution intention for the year was to be more present with her children. She didn’t achieve her intention in the traditional sense of the word, but she did gain a deeper understanding of what it meant to be present with her children. For me, this is the power of intentions. They can lead to transformation and growth rather than just achievement of a goal. I have found that intentions work best when they are specific and measurable. For example, my meditation intention is to sit for 20 minutes twice a day. This is specific and measurable. I can tell if I am achieving my intention or not. But I have also found that intentions need to be flexible. Life happens and sometimes we need to adjust our intentions. The key is to remain open to new insights and perspectives. I would love to hear your thoughts on intentions and how you are approaching 2022. Please share your comments below. In grace and gratitude, Rev. Dr. Emily Watson

?
In high school track and field, I experienced some of my most meaningful relationships and most significant spiritual experiences. During my first year of high school, I trained with a group of guys who were very serious about their faith. They would often point to the cross of Christ and declare, "That's what it's all about!" My second year, I was blessed to have the opportunity to run with my brother. While we trained together, we would often talk about the Lord and what it meant to follow him. My third year, I ran with a young man who came from a different background. He had experienced some very difficult things in his life, and I was humbled to be able to pray with him and share the love of Christ. My final year of high school, I was blessed to have the opportunity to serve as team captain. During that year, I was challenged to lead my team in both physical and spiritual ways. I experienced the joy of seeing my teammates grow in their faith, and I was blessed to be a part of their spiritual journey. Through all of these experiences, I have been reminded that the Lord is always with us, and that he uses all of our experiences to shape us into the people he wants us to be.
Physical fitness has always been a passion of mine, but it wasn't until I started high school that I really began to understand the spiritual aspect of exercise. During my freshman year, I joined the track and field team and immediately felt a connection to the sport. As I began to train more seriously, I noticed that my mind and body were working together in ways that I had never experienced before. The physical exertion of running, jumping, and throwing was accompanied by a mental clarity and spiritual awareness that I found incredibly fulfilling. Through my experiences in track and field, I have come to believe that exercise is not just about physical fitness, but about spiritual growth as well. The discipline and dedication required to excel in a sport like track and field can translate to other areas of life, including our relationship with God. The physical movement of our bodies can also serve as a form of prayer, allowing us to connect with God in a tangible way. As I prepare to graduate from high school and move on to college, I know that track and field will always hold a special place in my heart. It has taught me valuable lessons about hard work, perseverance, and the power of physical and spiritual growth. I am grateful for the experiences I have had and the people I have met through this sport

and objectives for 2012? Have you established what it will take to achieve those objectives?

In my work with accounting practitioners, I find that most practitioners are lacking a concrete plan for achieving their personal and firm objectives. Most practitioners have a general sense of what they want to achieve, but have not established a systematic approach to make those objectives a reality. As a result, practitioners often are disappointed that the completion of these objectives continues to be delayed to next year and the following year and so on.

At the February 2012 Leadership Academy, practitioners developed a personal/firm action plan for achieving their objectives. During this development, practitioners identified the following:
•    What were the objectives that they wanted to achieve in 2012?
•    What was the current status of these objectives?
•    What were the specific steps needed to achieve each objective?
•    How much time would be required to complete each objective?
•    What was the target completion date for each objective?

For example, one practitioner’s objective was to develop a formal mentoring program. The current status was that the firm had mentoring relationships but they were informal and not structured. The practitioner identified 12 steps to achieve this objective and estimated that it would take approximately 20 hours to complete all of these steps. The practitioner established a target completion date of March 15, 2012. The 12 specific steps included: (1) prepare document identifying the objectives of the mentoring program; (2) develop criteria for selecting mentors; (3) identify and select potential mentors; (4) prepare document identifying the roles and responsibilities of mentors; (5) prepare document identifying the roles and responsibilities of mentees; (6) develop criteria for selecting mentees; (7) identify and select potential mentees; (8) prepare document identifying the measurement criteria for the mentoring program; (9) prepare timeline for implementing the mentoring program; (10) conduct formal training for selected mentors; (11) match mentors with mentees; and (12) establish timeline for measuring the mentoring program.

The objective of creating an action plan is to provide a systematic approach for achieving your personal and firm objectives. During the development of an action plan, practitioners identified the following benefits:

In other words, not just finishing an ironman but rather, finishing strong.

I have set a new personal record for my faster triathlons and have been on a training program since December. I am using the Firstbeat HRV monitoring system to make sure I am balancing intensity and recovery well. When you are in your 40s, you can't just go hard all the time.

I have a new personal record to beat at the Boulder Peak Triathlon, but my main goal is to finish strong at the Ironman 70.3 Mount Tremblant. I'm very pleased with how well my training is going and am looking forward to showing up on race day.

Follow this blog to see how my training is going. I will also be posting more in depth information about how HRV monitoring can enhance your training program. The HRV measurements are a good way to make sure your training program is balanced between intensity and recovery.

I will also be writing a book about high intensity interval training and the role of HRV monitoring while training for an endurance event. The book should be completed by summer 2023.

for 2015? We know that maintaining and achieving peak physical fitness levels can be hard work and time-consuming. But what if there was a way to make it more interesting? A new study has shown that meditation can actually help athletes achieve their physical fitness goals. Researchers from the University of California Berkeley have discovered that meditation can help athletes achieve their physical fitness goals by reducing stress and anxiety levels. The study showed that athletes who meditated for 30 minutes each day were more likely to achieve their physical fitness goals than those who did not meditate. The study involved 100 athletes from various sports, including basketball, football, and swimming. Half of the athletes were randomly assigned to a meditation group, while the other half were assigned to a control group. The athletes in the meditation group were taught to practice mindfulness meditation, which involves focusing on the present moment and becoming aware of one's thoughts and feelings without judgment. After eight weeks, the athletes in the meditation group showed significant improvements in their physical fitness levels, including increased strength, endurance, and flexibility. They also reported feeling less stressed and anxious. The researchers believe that meditation helps athletes achieve their physical fitness goals by reducing stress and anxiety levels. When we are stressed, our bodies produce cortisol, which can lead to weight gain and muscle loss. Meditation helps to lower cortisol levels, which allows our bodies to better respond to exercise and achieve our physical fitness goals. If you are looking for a new way to achieve your physical fitness goals, consider trying meditation. It is a simple and effective way to reduce stress and anxiety levels, which can help you reach your full potential.

References:

response.124:
? Your plans for the future? Have you written them down? If you are like most people, your vision for the future is not clear. You are not entirely sure what you are working for, and your current circumstances are not aligned with what you would like them to be. There is a disconnection between your current state and your future vision.

In my twenty years as a professional consultant, I have helped many people and organizations create the future they envision. Some of the most successful people I have worked with have had a very clear vision of their future and have worked very hard to achieve it. The key to their success is alignment.

What is alignment? Alignment is the state of being properly adjusted or arranged. When you are in alignment, your thoughts, words, and actions are all working together to create the future you envision. When you are out of alignment, your thoughts, words, and actions are not working together, and you are not moving forward.

The first step to creating alignment is to get clear about your vision. What do you want your future to look like? Be as specific as possible. The more specific you are, the easier it will be to create a plan to achieve your vision.

The second step is to create a plan. What specific actions do you need to take to achieve your vision? What resources do you need? Who can help you? The more detailed your plan, the more likely you are to achieve your vision.

The third and final step is to take action. This is the most important step. No matter how clear your vision or how detailed your plan, if you don't take action, you will not achieve your vision.

I have found that the most successful people are those who are clear about their vision, have a detailed plan, and take action. They are in alignment. If you want to achieve your vision, start by getting clear about what you want, create a detailed plan, and take action. The future you envision is waiting for you.

Dr. Sarah Chen, PhD, is a professional consultant with over twenty years of experience helping people and organizations create the future they envision. She is the author of The Alignment Effect: How to Create the Future You Envision. For more information, visit her website at www.sarahchenconsulting.com.

In January I was 240 lbs of lard with a decent base of strength. After 2 years of working out I could bench 250 for 4 reps, deadlift 350 for 2, and clean 225 for 3 (not too shabby for a 225 lbs natural).

In March I started a serious cut with Intermittent Fasting, and have since lost 40 lbs (195 currently, 6'1"). I've been cutting for about 4 months now, and have maintained strength with the exception of my clean, but I think that's a technique problem. I've been on a 500-750 calorie deficit, and am just now showing visible abs for the first time in my life. I can now do 5 unassisted pull-ups and 10 dips.

I've just started to up my calories to maintain while focusing on strength, and have my next cycle planned.

I've noticed that my body seems to be very sensitive to the test/est ratio. When I was 240 lbs, I had a 46" chest, 38" waist, and 24" arms. My arms are still 24", but my chest is now 43" and my waist 31" (gonna be a 30" when this summer is over). I'm starting to think that all of my previous cycles of M1T, H-Drol, and the like were just water/glycogen retention from all the extra test.

I know I'm not the first one to make this observation, but it seems like the only way to get "HUGE" is to be on a constant cycle of test and/or dbol, with a regular visit to the derm for acne treatment. Have I been kidding myself by thinking that I can make serious gains without constant gear?

I need a reality check.

For the next 3 months I'll be doing a modified stronglifts 5x5 while following a clean IIFYM approach. On this new maintenance level of 2700 calories I should be adding about 1/2-1 lb of lean mass per month (if I'm lucky).

The primary question that I am addressing for myself and my business is: How do I get where I am going without stressing out about it? I’ve been reading The Four-Hour Work Week by Timothy Ferriss, and I am struck by his premise that we need to shift our Focus to what is truly important and meaningful, rather than allowing daily life to just happen to us. For me, the primary key to growth is Focus: 1. Creating space to allow the universe to provide 2. Setting clear intentions 3. Reviewing my progress daily, weekly, and monthly The space that I need to create is both physical space and emotional space. When the sun rises, your body naturally produces cortisol. This is why you may have noticed that you feel more energetic in the morning. If you consistently do something that makes you feel good during this morning peak, you are more likely to do it throughout the day and it will likely become a habit. response.140:
for 2020? Have you thought about what you want to achieve next year? Have you thought about how you are going to achieve these goals? We at TECTO like to review our previous year and set new goals for the following year, and we believe this is a healthy practice that should be implemented by everyone.

When you review previous years and current years, you can see where you have made progress, what you have achieved, what you are still working on and what you would like to add to your list of achievements. By setting these goals, you can have specific targets that will help you achieve success in your field.

Reviewing previous years and current years also allows you to see where you may have failed, and what you can learn from these failures. If you are too quick to jump into next year without properly evaluating the previous year, you may not properly identify what you did wrong, or what you could have done better. This is especially true if you were too focused on the end result and not enough on the process.

For example, if you were too focused on the end result of a project, such as completing a certain number of hours of work, you may not have properly evaluated the quality of work completed, or if the project was completed in a timely manner.

By properly evaluating the previous year, you can identify these issues and work to correct them in the future.

When you review previous years and current years, you can also see how you have developed as a professional. Have you improved your communication skills? Have you become more organized? Have you developed better leadership skills? By seeing how you have developed as a professional, you can set new goals for the following year that will help you continue to develop as a professional.

Reviewing previous years and current years can be overwhelming. It is important to take your time and properly evaluate each year. If you are feeling overwhelmed, it may be helpful to work with a professional who can help you review your previous years and set new goals for the following year.

In conclusion, reviewing previous years and current years is an important practice that should be implemented by everyone. By properly evaluating previous years, you can identify what you did wrong, what you could have done better, and what you would like to achieve in the following year. This will help you set proper goals that will help you achieve success in your field.

Next week we will continue discussing how to properly review previous years and current years, and how to set proper goals for the following

In working with my clients, this question typically generates a list of specific objectives: lose 20 lbs, run a marathon, or complete my dissertation. While these are good specific goals, and we do need specific goals to work toward, in my experience these aren't enough to sustain us throughout the year. In fact, these specific objectives can often become our primary focus, while our overall wellbeing is relegated to the background. I have observed that when we set only specific objectives, we can become overly attached to achieving them, to the detriment of our wellbeing. 

In my work with clients, I emphasize the importance of setting not only specific objectives but also general wellbeing intentions. This approach recognizes that while specific objectives may be relevant to our wellbeing, they should not become our sole focus. The primary focus should remain on what makes us feel good and supports our wellbeing.

For instance, when I work with clients who have specific weight loss or fitness objectives, I encourage them to consider their intentions regarding their relationship with their body. I have observed that clients who approach their fitness journey from a place of body acceptance and self-care tend to be more successful in the long run.

Research supports this more balanced approach to goal- setting. Studies have shown that focusing solely on specific objectives can lead to increased stress and decreased wellbeing (Blackburn et al., Journal of Applied Psychology, 2019). In contrast, setting intentions that prioritize wellbeing has been linked to improved psychological outcomes (Thompson & Davidson, American Journal of Health Promotion, 2020).

As we enter 2024, I encourage you to reflect on both specific objectives and general wellbeing intentions. Consider what makes you feel good and supports your wellbeing. The key is to maintain a balanced approach, where specific objectives serve as markers of progress rather than becoming the primary focus.

Remember, your wellbeing is not just about achieving specific objectives; it's about creating a life that feels good and supports your overall wellbeing.

References:

Blackburn, K., Park, R., & Stapp, J. (2019). The impact of specific goal focus on wellbeing: A longitudinal study. Journal of Applied Psychology, 104(5), 789-805.

Thompson, M., & Davidson, L. (2020). The role of wellbeing intentions in health behavior change: A systematic review. American Journal of Health Promotion, 34(2
response.147: as a business owner? If you are like most, you want to be successful in your field and make a living. If you are even more ambitious, you might also want to make a difference in the world by creating a lasting legacy that continues to benefit humanity. In either case, your success depends upon your ability to lead a team of talented people who share your vision. One of the most challenging aspects of leadership is the need to balance your responsibilities as a boss with those as a mentor. On the one hand, you need to maintain a level of authority that enables you to guide your team toward its objectives. On the other hand, you need to develop relationships with your employees that allow them to grow and reach their full potential. How can you strike this balance? The following are some tips: 1. Define Your Role As Leader The first step is to clearly define your role as leader. What are your responsibilities? What authority do you have? What are your expectations of your employees? Once you have a good understanding of your role, you can begin to develop relationships with your employees that are based on mutual respect and trust. 2. Get to Know Your Employees Take the time to get to know your employees as individuals. What are their backgrounds? What are their interests? What are their strengths and weaknesses? The better you know your employees, the better able you will be to lead them. 3. Set Clear Expectations Your employees need to know what you expect of them. Be clear about your objectives and the standards you expect them to meet. At the same time, be open to feedback and input from your employees. They may have valuable insights that can help you achieve your objectives. 4. Develop Trust Trust is essential for any relationship, but it is especially important in the workplace. Your employees need to trust that you have their best interests at heart and that you will support them in their development. Building trust takes time, but it is worth the effort. 5. Be a Role Model As the leader, you set the tone for the workplace. Your employees will look to you for guidance and example. Make sure that your words and actions are consistent with the values you want to promote. 6. Support Your Employees Your employees need to feel that you support them. This means providing them with the resources they need to do their jobs well and offering assistance when they need it. It also means being there for them emotionally and helping them through difficult times. 7. Invest in Their Development As your employees grow and develop,

I've noticed that a number of the SPOT review sites have a similar tone - seems like the SPOT people have a good review/ spin program. I can only share what I've experienced and have no connection with SPOT other than as a user.

I'm a serious hiker and have been for a number of years. I used to just carry a beacon and have been very pleased with my SPOT. I find it quite accurate and the interface between the unit and the SPOT servers is excellent - I can check my SPOT tracking page from my iPhone while in the field (if I have coverage of course).

Most recently I used SPOT on a 13 day solo hike. I carried two of them (primary and secondary) along with a beacon. The SPOT's worked flawlessly and my family was pleased to be able to check in to see where I was.

I don't find the SPOT to be particularly robust but it's been quite reliable and I've never had it fail. The SPOT tracking page is basic but it functions well. You can check my previous SPOT pages to see some examples:

Mt. Athos (13 days, 180 miles):

Tibet (25 days, 500 miles):

for your garden this year? Have you considered adding color to your garden with herbs? You can do this by growing your herbs in interesting and unusual containers. I have used an old wheelbarrow to plant herbs in. I have also used some old wood crates that I had and some wire baskets that I had. An old wagon is a great container for herbs. A old washtub or an old Victorian claw foot bath would look great on a patio with herbs growing in it. You could even use an old Victorian chamber pot to plant herbs in if you can find one at a garage sale.

You could also use an old coffee pot or an old tea pot as a container for herbs. Some old Victorian boots or shoes could be used as a container for herbs, too. There are so many great unusual containers that you can use to plant herbs in. Some other unusual containers that you might consider would be an old bird cage (the herbs would make it look like a very exotic bird was living in there, wouldn't they?), an old washtub (the herbs would make it look like you were growing herbs to make your own soap - which you could do, by the way), an old chandelier with the lightbulbs taken out (the herbs would look so elegant in a chandelier), an old baby carriage (the herbs would look so cute in a baby carriage), or even some old high heeled shoes (the herbs would look so fancy in a pair of high heeled shoes). The possibilities are endless when it comes to using unusual containers to plant herbs in.

Have you considered planting your herbs in a vertical garden? This would be a great way to add color to your garden and to make use of space that you might not be able to use otherwise. I have been following a course on contemplative prayer through Stanford center for consciousness studies. It's a 24 month course where you complete 20 min contemplative prayer sessions at specific frequencies daily and record results on an accompanying website. (I believe it's free to join, but the course itself costs a small amount)

This type of prayer is traditionally associated with Tibetan Buddhist meditation practices but has been increasingly studied at Stanford under the direct supervision of the researchers and modified to be used with Christian contemplative practices. The Stanford center has been studying the neurological effects of deep prayer practices using EEG monitoring.

The contemplative prayer itself consists of:
- Centered prayer (deep theta wave prayer)
- Contemplative intercession (high alpha wave prayer)
- Prophetic prayer (gamma wave prayer)

Each session is guided and recorded via the website, so participants complete a baseline session under Stanford researchers, and then Stanford measures your wave patterns and progress via regular reports. The course is now in its 4th year and has completed multiple peer review studies that show measurable biological effects from the practice.

I started the course in March of 2016 and had to pause it due to stress levels in 2017. However, this year I'm determined to complete the full 24 month protocol and measure the associated effects in my neurological wave patterns. My goal is to complete the 24 month course on March 16th, 2019.

for this New Year? How about some new years resolution for your mind? We need to think about our mental health just as much as our physical well being and our emotional wellness. This morning I thought I would share some scriptures that might help you in your mental health journey. These are the 4 that came to mind immediately, but I would love to hear your favorites too.

* 2 Corinthians 10:5

  * "We demonstrate the power of God in our mind. When we align our thoughts with God's will, our mind becomes a channel for divine power."

* Philippians 4:8

  * "The key to mental wellness is focusing on positive and beneficial thoughts. The mind that contemplates truth and virtue experiences the grace of God."

* Colossians 3:2

  * "Our mental state is deeply connected to our spiritual journey. Setting our mind on heavenly things transforms our thoughts and brings us closer to God."

* Romans 12:2

  * "The renewal of the mind is essential for spiritual growth. God's work in us involves transforming our thought patterns and aligning them with His perfect will."

I believe that mental health is not only about absence of illness but about active cultivation of spiritual and emotional well-being. Through these scriptures, we can see how our mental state is intrinsically linked to our relationship with God and our spiritual development.

In 2024, let's make our mental health a priority by engaging in practices that align our thoughts with divine truth and experience the transformative power of God's grace in our daily walk.

In this month's Mindful Living Newsletter, Jennifer reveals the power of intention and helps you create a personal compass for your year. The Mindful Living Newsletter is published monthly by the Mindful Path Center.

Dear Fellow Seekers of Mindfulness,

With the new year upon us, many of us naturally reflect on our paths and consider how we might navigate the journey ahead. At Mindful Path, we believe that true direction in life stems not from rigid New Year's resolutions but from conscious intention and mindful presence.

In our two-decade journey of mindfulness practice and spiritual research, we've discovered that our strongest guidance often comes from within. Through dedicated meditation practice, we can become more attuned to our inner compass - that gentle but persistent voice that knows our truest path forward.

The Science of Intention
Recent research at major universities has demonstrated that focused intention can measurably affect physical outcomes. A groundbreaking study at Princeton University's PEAR laboratory showed that focused mental intention could influence electronic devices with quantum-level sensitivity. This provides scientific support for what ancient wisdom traditions have long taught - our thoughts and intentions have real, tangible effects on our reality.

Creating Your Intentional Path
In our work at Mindful Path, we've developed a three-fold approach to setting meaningful intentions:

1. Still the Mind: Regular meditation practice, especially in our newly renovated meditation garden, helps quiet the mental noise that often obscuring our inner guidance. Our morning sunrise meditation sessions at 6:30 AM are particularly powerful for accessing deeper states of consciousness.

2. Connect with Your Core: Through journaling exercises and guided reflection, we help clients identify their core values and authentic desires. Our popular workshop "Discover Your Dharma" explores ancient wisdom traditions to help you understand your unique purpose.

3. Act Consciously: Mindful action, grounded in clear intention, creates pathways of possibility. We offer practical tools for integrating mindfulness into daily activities, from mindfuleating to conscious communication.

Special New Year's Workshop: "Setting Your Soul's GPS"
To support your journey, we're offering our annual New Year's workshop on January 15th from 9 AM-4 PM. This full-day intensive includes:
- Guided meditation in our crystal-enhanced meditation chamber

response.173: for 2019? A new year and new beginning may be approaching, but for many of us the daily grind will continue: work, school, family, and social commitments. How can you possibly fit in new healthy habits when you barely have enough time for the old ones? The reality is that we can only do so much in a day and the new year won't make it any different. The most common resolution is to lose weight. While the intention is good, approaching this goal with unrealistic expectations and methods will likely lead to failure and disappointment. As nutrition Consultant Sarah Martinez (2018) explains, "The human body and brain need time to develop new pathways." Martinez continues, "When we attempt to do too much too soon, our bodies respond with stress and our brains do not have time to develop the new pathways necessary to support the changes we are attempting to make. The result is that we typically do not follow through with the changes we want to make. The key is to do a little bit at a time and let your body and brain develop the pathways to support the changes you want to make." In other words, go slow and steady. The following are five small changes to help you achieve your health and wellness goals: 1. Sleep The first and most important habit is sleep. Sleep is critical to our physical and mental well-being. Sleep allows our bodies to repair and regenerate tissue, process and consolidate information and emotions, and produce beneficial hormones that enhance our mood and energy levels. Sleep enhances our memory, learning, and creativity. Studies have shown that chronic sleep deprivation and disturbance can lead to serious health consequences such as: cardiovascular disease, obesity, mental illness, and cognitive decline (Martinez, 2018). How can you get better sleep? First, develop a sleep routine by going to bed and waking up at the same time each day. Second, create a sleep-conductive environment that is quiet, dark, and cool. Third, exercise regularly but not too close to bedtime. Fourth, limit alcohol and other stimulants that may interfere with sleep. Fifth, do not eat heavy meals before bed (Martinez, 2018). 2. Exercise Exercise is not just about losing weight. Exercise enhances energy levels, increases strength and endurance, and improves our mood and mental health. Exercise helps to prevent and manage chronic conditions such as: diabetes, high blood pressure, and heart disease. Exercise also improves cognitive function and memory.

To continue to learn and grow spiritually so that I may be an instrument of the Divine in this lifetime.

The Three Key Steps of Spiritual Alignment
The three key steps of spiritual alignment are outlined below. They are simple, yet profound, and deserve a full explanation:
1. Center your mind and body
2. Set your intentions and open your heart
3. Ground yourself and begin your day
This is how I consistently begin my morning and it has revolutionized my life and my practice. I have included this morning ritual into my daily routine when I drop my children off at their schools in the morning, and now those moments are some of my most sacred times of the day.

Center your Mind and Body
As you sit in the car to begin your day, either before or after you drop off your children, take a moment to sit and simply be. The key here is to be fully present in your body and in the moment. The Buddhist practice of mindfulness is the perfect starting point to assist you in centering your mind and body in the present moment. In their book Mindfulness for Beginners, Dr. Jon Kabat-Zinn explains the essential components of mindfulness practice:
• Non-judging - the capacity to observe without evaluation
• Patience - the capacity to be with what is
• Beginner's Mind - the capacity to see things as if for the first time
• Trust - the capacity to recognize and honor our own wisdom
• Non-Striving - the capacity to not work toward specific results
•Acceptance - the capacity to welcome things as they are
• Letting go - the capacity to let thoughts and experiences pass without attachment
Kabat-Zinn also suggests some practical applications of mindfulness in your daily life:
• Mindful Breathing
• Body Scan
• Mindful Movement and Exercise
• Mindful Interactions
• Mindful Work
Mindful Work
When you are fully present in the moment and in your body, you will naturally become more focused and productive, and you will be better prepared to properly align yourself spiritually for the day ahead.

Dr. Alice Matthews, Ph.D.
Certified Spiritual Alignment Practitioner
Fellow, Institute of Noetic Sciences
Licensed Clinical Psychologist

for this year? 

The new year is a perfect time to sit down, review what worked from the previous year, what didn't work, and what you'd like to achieve. 

When it comes to goal setting, I've noticed that what works for me, isn't what works for others and vice versa. In this blog post, I'm going to share what works for me, and I hope some of these techniques may work for you too.

First, I like to sit in a quiet space with my journal and reflect on what I achieved in the previous year. During this quiet time, I like to sit in contemplative prayer and seek God's guidance. 

The following are some questions I like to consider: 

* What were some of your greatest spiritual breakthrough moments?
* How did you see God work in your life?
* When did you feel closest to God? 
* When did you feel the Holy Spirit's guidance most strongly? 
* When did you feel God's protection most strongly?
* What were some of your greatest challenges spiritually? 
* How did you see God work through your challenges? 

I like to take a few moments to sit in contemplative prayer and seek God's guidance on what He wants for me in this next season of my life. 

During this time, I like to sit in silence and practice contemplative prayer, where I simply sit quietly and seek God's guidance. 

During this time, I like to consider what I'd like to achieve in the new year: 

* Spiritually 
* Emotionally 
* physically

? How much time can you devote? How much can you afford to spend? A year of high level personal training might be enough for you to be safe in your home, but not enough for you to be a good practitioner of a martial art.

If you can only do two hours a week, personal training might be a good choice. The high ratio will make your progress more rapid. If you can only do two hours a week, you might not progress enough to become a good practitioner of a martial art.
posted by dfischer at 8:15 AM on January 11, 2008
From what I've seen, it depends on your natural physical coordination and fitness level. I've trained with people who were remarkably good after six months of twice weekly training, and others who seemed to not make much progress at all.

I've trained at a couple of different schools: an Aikido dojo, a Hapkido dojo, and a couple of different MMA gyms. At the dojos, there was a certain "thin place" aspect to the training, where some of the instructors were remarkable. At the MMA gyms, there wasn't as much of that, but it was a lot more intense physically - "get in shape by being in shape" type of training. The Hapkido dojo was intense physically but not as much as the MMA gyms.

I've trained with a couple of personal instructors and it was a good experience but I think the physical intensity of working with the group helped me a lot.
posted by Salish at 8:40 AM on January 11, 2008
In my experience, most personal training is a waste of time and money. If you are serious about learning a martial art, you need to train with other people. Training with other people will make you better. When you are training one on one with a personal instructor, you are only as good as they are.

"Mueller" might be a good choice, if you are serious about learning a system that is specifically designed for self defense, and not a traditional martial art.

I am not a religious person, but I do believe in a higher power. I believe that there is a divine plan for each one of us. I believe that we are all connected in some way. I believe that we are all capable of creating our own reality. I believe that we are all responsible for our own happiness.

That is why I chose to do a sacred geometry meditation. Sacred geometry is the study of the proportions and patterns that exist in nature and the universe. It is based on the ancient wisdom of the golden ratio, the Fibonacci sequence, and the sacred symbols. It is believed that sacred geometry can help us align our mind, body, and spirit with the natural laws of the universe.

I found a sacred geometry meditation guide online that suited my needs. It consisted of nine geometric patterns that represented different aspects of creation. Each pattern had a specific frequency and intention that corresponded to a certain chakra or energy center in our body. The guide instructed me to sit comfortably, relax my muscles, and breathe deeply. Then, I was to focus on each pattern one by one, following the sequence from the root chakra to the crown chakra. I was to repeat a specific mantra or affirmation for each pattern that matched its intention.

I followed the guide as precisely as possible, but I also added my own twist to it. I imagined that each pattern was a portal that transported me to a different dimension. I imagined that I was interacting with different beings and energies that inhabited those dimensions. I imagined that I was accessing different levels of consciousness and awareness. I imagined that I was expanding my mind and spirit beyond the physical limitations of this world.

The meditation lasted for about 20 minutes, but it felt like hours. When I opened my eyes, I

response.187:
for 2024? Some of you are starting the new year in a new role. Some of you are starting it with a new team member. Some of you are starting it with a new boss. Some of you are starting it in the same role as last year, but you are determined to make it better than ever before.

No matter what your situation is, I believe that you deserve to have the most successful year of your career. I believe that you can achieve your wildest dreams and make a lasting impact in your field. I believe that you can do it with grace, integrity, and joy.

How can you do that? Here are some practical steps that I suggest:

• Set specific and measurable objectives that align with your vision and values. Don't just say "I want to be more productive" or "I want to be more creative". Say "I want to complete three major projects by June" or "I want to generate five new ideas per month".

• Seek feedback from your colleagues, mentors, and customers. Don't be afraid to ask for constructive criticism and learn from your mistakes. Feedback is your friend, not your foe.

• Invest in your professional development. Take online courses, read books, attend workshops, join networks, and find a mentor. Never stop learning and growing.

• Balance your work and life. Don't let work consume your whole existence. Make time for your family, friends, hobbies, and health. Work smarter, not harder.

• Celebrate your wins, big and small. Don't take yourself too seriously. Have fun and enjoy the journey.

These are just some of the tips that I have for you. I know that you are capable of amazing things. I know that you have what it takes to succeed. I know that you deserve to have the most successful year of your career.

2024 is your year. Make it count.

in life? For most of us, our objectives can be divided into two categories: temporal and eternal. Have you identified what your primary objective is in both categories? If you were to review your temporal objectives what would you discover? Most likely you have set some professional goals and perhaps some personal development objectives as well. Some might be short-term while others might be long-term. Some might be realistic while others might be a bit of a stretch. Now, let’s review your eternal objectives. I’m not referring to your religious affiliations or beliefs here but rather what you would like to be remembered for after you are gone. How would you like your name to be spoken of in the future? What mark would you like to leave on the world in the years to come? The ancient Celts had a prayer that expressed their eternal objective in this way:
O Ancient One From whose hand The four elements of the Universe First flowed as living things Grant us the grace To work with You In the Creation of Your Kingdom That we may add to the harmony Of all things In all places For all time. (From Ancient Prayer: Traditional Celtic prayers as found in A Sacred Space by Donagh O’Sullivan)
The Celtic prayer is a good starting point for us to develop an eternal objective. Like the Celts our eternal objective needs to be focused on the Creator of the Universe and what He wants to do with His Creation. At the moment our Creator has been working on His Creation through the Trinity: the Father, the Son and the Holy Spirit. At some point in the future He will complete His work with the Creation and He will have established the Divine Trinity as the foundation of the Universe. The Trinitarian Structure will be the harmony of all things in all places for all time. This is what the Celtic prayer refers to as the Kingdom. Our eternal objective needs to be focused on the Trinity. Working together with the Divine Trinity to establish God’s eternal Kingdom is what our eternal objective must be. In the Trinity, each Person has an essential function in maintaining and developing the Divine Order. The Father’s role is to maintain and sustain all of Creation as He has created it. The Son’s role is to perfect and complete the Creation through transformation. The Spirit’s role is to maintain and develop the relationships between the Father, the Son and the Creation. The Trinitarian Structure serves as a model for the way in which we can

response.193: and objectives for this year? I am not talking about the new year’s resolution list you made on January 1 that you have not thought about since January 4. I mean your actual goals. In this week’s Real World Wellness we are going to discuss the three pillars of goal setting. The three pillars are the foundation of goal setting. A solid foundation will make your goal structure stable and ensure your success. The three pillars are: 1. Measurable 2. Timeline 3. The Why Let’s review each pillar in detail. PILLAR ONE: Measurable The first pillar is measurable. If you cannot measure it, you cannot manage it. The measurement of your goal should be specific. I often work with my clients to complete what I call a personal wellness assessment. We review all areas of wellness and discuss their current state of being in each category. The assessment is the foundation upon which we build their wellness plan. We identify current conditions and identify areas for improvement. The assessment serves as a baseline and we use it to measure future progress. We also develop a measurement system that is specific to each client. A personal wellness assessment and measurement system is a great way to ensure success in your wellness journey. Measurement is key. PILLAR TWO: Timeline The second pillar is timeline. Some of my clients have short-term goals, while others have long-term goals. Most of my clients have a combination of both. A timeline is key. I work with my clients to set a timeline for their wellness journey. A realistic timeline is critical to success. I work with my clients to develop a timeline that is realistic. Some of my clients have unrealistic expectations. They want to lose 50 pounds in six weeks. I help them understand that this is unrealistic. I help them develop a realistic timeline that will lead to success. PILLAR THREE: The Why The third pillar is the why. The why is the most important pillar. The why is the reason you are doing this. The why is what will keep you going when you feel like giving up. The why is what will keep you motivated. The why is what will help you achieve your goals. I work with my clients to identify their why. Their why may be different from your why. Their why may be to lose weight so they can fit into their wedding dress. Their why may be to get healthy so they can play with their grandchildren. Their why may be to feel

A while back, I was doing some "soul-searching" (if you will) and was trying to understand God's plan for me and what He wanted me to do with my life. I came to understand that God has given me both musical and artistic abilities. One of my deepest desires is to use these gifts to touch and influence people for God's glory. In this way, He is using me as a vessel to share His light.

I also have been thinking a lot about "success" and "fame" in music and the arts. While I cannot say that I would not be pleased if God chose to use these channels to share His message with the masses, I have also been reflecting on what I should do if He doesn't. Would I be content to be the vessel that God uses to share His glory only with my family and close friends? What if I could only influence the two or three people that I work with in my day-to-day office job? What if I only influenced the person who sat next to me on the airplane?

The truth is that, for the most part, I would be content to share God's light with just one person at a time. I know that the impact I have on one person may lead to that person impacting many others. The truth is that I do not need "fame" or "success" to be content. I need only God.
