To make a real difference to children who are facing challenges that would make most adults give up.
To be a consistent role model for my two children and to make them see the value of serving others.
To continue to learn and grow spiritually so that I may be an instrument of the Divine in this lifetime.

The Three Key Steps of Spiritual Alignment
The three key steps of spiritual alignment are outlined below. They are simple, yet profound, and deserve a full explanation:
1. Center your mind and body
2. Set your intentions and open your heart
3. Ground yourself and begin your day
This is how I consistently begin my morning and it has revolutionized my life and my practice. I have included this morning ritual into my daily routine when I drop my children off at their schools in the morning, and now those moments are some of my most sacred times of the day.

Center your Mind and Body
As you sit in the car to begin your day, either before or after you drop off your children, take a moment to sit and simply be. The key here is to be fully present in your body and in the moment. The Buddhist practice of mindfulness is the perfect starting point to assist you in centering your mind and body in the present moment. In their book Mindfulness for Beginners, Dr. Jon Kabat-Zinn explains the essential components of mindfulness practice:
• Non-judging - the capacity to observe without evaluation
• Patience - the capacity to be with what is
• Beginner's Mind - the capacity to see things as if for the first time
• Trust - the capacity to recognize and honor our own wisdom
• Non-Striving - the capacity to not work toward specific results
•Acceptance - the capacity to welcome things as they are
• Letting go - the capacity to let thoughts and experiences pass without attachment
Kabat-Zinn also suggests some practical applications of mindfulness in your daily life:
• Mindful Breathing
• Body Scan
• Mindful Movement and Exercise
• Mindful Interactions
• Mindful Work
Mindful Work
When you are fully present in the moment and in your body, you will naturally become more focused and productive, and you will be better prepared to properly align yourself spiritually for the day ahead.

for this year? 

The new year is a perfect time to sit down, review what worked from the previous year, what didn't work, and what you'd like to achieve. 

When it comes to goal setting, I've noticed that what works for me, isn't what works for others and vice versa. In this blog post, I'm going to share what works for me, and I hope some of these techniques may work for you too.

First, I like to sit in a quiet space with my journal and reflect on what I achieved in the previous year. During this quiet time, I like to sit in contemplative prayer and seek God's guidance. 

The following are some questions I like to consider: 

* What were some of your greatest spiritual breakthrough moments?
* How did you see God work in your life?
* When did you feel closest to God? 
* When did you feel the Holy Spirit's guidance most strongly? 
* When did you feel God's protection most strongly?
* What were some of your greatest challenges spiritually? 
* How did you see God work through your challenges? 

I like to take a few moments to sit in contemplative prayer and seek God's guidance on what He wants for me in this next season of my life. 

During this time, I like to sit in silence and practice contemplative prayer, where I simply sit quietly and seek God's guidance. 

During this time, I like to consider what I'd like to achieve in the new year: 

* Spiritually 
* Emotionally 
* physically 
* professionally 
* relationally 

I like to write these down in my journal, and then I like to sit in contemplative prayer and seek God's guidance on what He wants for me in this next season of my life.

? How much time can you devote? How much can you afford to spend? A year of high level personal training might be enough for you to be safe in your home, but not enough for you to be a good practitioner of a martial art.

If you can only do two hours a week, personal training might be a good choice. The high ratio will make your progress more rapid. If you can only do two hours a week, you might not progress enough to become a good practitioner of a martial art.
posted by dfischer at 8:15 AM on January 11, 2008
From what I've seen, it depends on your natural physical coordination and fitness level. I've trained with people who were remarkably good after six months of twice weekly training, and others who seemed to not make much progress at all.

I've trained at a couple of different schools: an Aikido dojo, a Hapkido dojo, and a couple of different MMA gyms. At the dojos, there was a certain "thin place" aspect to the training, where some of the instructors were remarkable. At the MMA gyms, there wasn't as much of that, but it was a lot more intense physically - "get in shape by being in shape" type of training. The Hapkido dojo was intense physically but not as much as the MMA gyms.

I've trained with a couple of personal instructors and it was a good experience but I think the physical intensity of working with the group helped me a lot.
posted by Salish at 8:40 AM on January 11, 2008
In my experience, most personal training is a waste of time and money. If you are serious about learning a martial art, you need to train with other people. Training with other people will make you better. When you are training one on one with a personal instructor, you are only as good as they are.

"Mueller" might be a good choice, if you are serious about learning a system that is specifically designed for self defense, and not a traditional martial art.

For references and recommendations - what are you looking for? Have you trained anywhere before? Have you trained in other systems? What are you looking to achieve?

That is why I chose to do a sacred geometry meditation. Sacred geometry is the study of the proportions and patterns that exist in nature and the universe. It is based on the ancient wisdom of the golden ratio, the Fibonacci sequence, and the sacred symbols. It is believed that sacred geometry can help us align our mind, body, and spirit with the natural laws of the universe.

I found a sacred geometry meditation guide online that suited my needs. It consisted of nine geometric patterns that represented different aspects of creation. Each pattern had a specific frequency and intention that corresponded to a certain chakra or energy center in our body. The guide instructed me to sit comfortably, relax my muscles, and breathe deeply. Then, I was to focus on each pattern one by one, following the sequence from the root chakra to the crown chakra. I was to repeat a specific mantra or affirmation for each pattern that matched its intention.

I followed the guide as precisely as possible, but I also added my own twist to it. I imagined that each pattern was a portal that transported me to a different dimension. I imagined that I was expanding my mind and spirit beyond the physical limitations of this world.

for 2024? Some of you are starting the new year in a new role. Some of you are starting it with a new team member. Some of you are starting it with a new boss. Some of you are starting it in the same role as last year, but you are determined to make it better than ever before.

No matter what your situation is, I believe that you deserve to have the most successful year of your career. I believe that you can achieve your wildest dreams and make a lasting impact in your field. I believe that you can do it with grace, integrity, and joy.

How can you do that? Here are some practical steps that I suggest:

• Set specific and measurable objectives that align with your vision and values. Don't just say "I want to be more productive" or "I want to be more creative". Say "I want to complete three major projects by June" or "I want to generate five new ideas per month".

• Seek feedback from your colleagues, mentors, and customers. Don't be afraid to ask for constructive criticism and learn from your mistakes. Feedback is your friend, not your foe.

• Invest in your professional development. Take online courses, read books, attend workshops, join networks, and find a mentor. Never stop learning and growing.

• Balance your work and life. Don't let work consume your whole existence. Make time for your family, friends, hobbies, and health. Work smarter, not harder.

• Celebrate your wins, big and small. Don't take yourself too seriously. Have fun and enjoy the journey.

These are just some of the tips that I have for you. I know that you are capable of amazing things. I know that you have what it takes to succeed. I know that you deserve to have the most successful year of your career.

2024 is your year. Make it count.

in life? For most of us, our objectives can be divided into two categories: temporal and eternal. Have you identified what your primary objective is in both categories? If you were to review your temporal objectives what would you discover? Most likely you have set some professional goals and perhaps some personal development objectives as well. Some might be short-term while others might be long-term. Some might be realistic while others might be a bit of a stretch. Now, let’s review your eternal objectives. I’m not referring to your religious affiliations or beliefs here but rather what you would like to be remembered for after you are gone. How would you like your name to be spoken of in the future? What mark would you like to leave on the world in the years to come? The ancient Celts had a prayer that expressed their eternal objective in this way:
O Ancient One From whose hand The four elements of the Universe First flowed as living things Grant us the grace To work with You In the Creation of Your Kingdom That we may add to the harmony Of all things In all places For all time. (From Ancient Prayer: Traditional Celtic prayers as found in A Sacred Space by Donagh O’Sullivan)
The Celtic prayer is a good starting point for us to develop an eternal objective. Like the Celts our eternal objective needs to be focused on the Creator of the Universe and what He wants to do with His Creation. At the moment our Creator has been working on His Creation through the Trinity: the Father, the Son and the Holy Spirit. At some point in the future He will complete His work with the Creation and He will have established the Divine Trinity as the foundation of the Universe. The Trinitarian Structure will be the harmony of all things in all places for all time. This is what the Celtic prayer refers to as the Kingdom. Our eternal objective needs to be focused on the Trinity. Working together with the Divine Trinity to establish God’s eternal Kingdom is what our eternal objective must be. In the Trinity, each Person has an essential function in maintaining and developing the Divine Order. The Father’s role is to maintain and sustain all of Creation as He has created it. The Son’s role is to perfect and complete the Creation through transformation. The Spirit’s role is to maintain and develop the relationships between the Father, the Son and the Creation. The Trinitarian Structure serves as a model for the way in which we can

updated_response.193: and objectives for this year? I am not talking about the new year’s resolution list you made on January 1 that you have not thought about since January 4. I mean your actual goals. In this week’s Real World Wellness we are going to discuss the three pillars of goal setting. The three pillars are the foundation of goal setting. A solid foundation will make your goal structure stable and ensure your success. The three pillars are: 1. Measurable 2. Timeline 3. The Why Let’s review each pillar in detail. PILLAR ONE: Measurable The first pillar is measurable. If you cannot measure it, you cannot manage it. The measurement of your goal should be specific. I often work with my clients to complete what I call a personal wellness assessment. We review all areas of wellness and discuss their current state of being in each category. The assessment is the foundation upon which we build their wellness plan. We identify current conditions and identify areas for improvement. The assessment serves as a baseline and we use it to measure future progress. We also develop a measurement system that is specific to each client. A personal wellness assessment and measurement system is a great way to ensure success in your wellness journey. Measurement is key. PILLAR TWO: Timeline The second pillar is timeline. Some of my clients have short-term goals, while others have long-term goals. Most of my clients have a combination of both. A timeline is key. I work with my clients to set a timeline for their wellness journey. A realistic timeline is critical to success. I work with my clients to develop a timeline that is realistic. Some of my clients have unrealistic expectations. They want to lose 50 pounds in six weeks. I help them understand that this is unrealistic. I help them develop a realistic timeline that will lead to success. PILLAR THREE: The Why The third pillar is the why. The why is the most important pillar. The why is the reason you are doing this. The why is what will keep you going when you feel like giving up. The why is what will keep you motivated. The why is what will help you achieve your goals. I work with my clients to identify their why. Their why may be different from your why. Their why may be to lose weight so they can fit into their wedding dress. ? Do you have any? How are you working toward them? How do you measure progress?

These are some of the questions that I am working on these days. I used to be so focused on work related goals and objectives. But, at 56 years old and following a major surgery that has given me a whole new perspective on life, I am approaching my personal and professional goals with a new energy.

In my 5 years of self-employment, I have become very focused on what I want and need to achieve from both a personal and professional perspective. As a solopreneur, what I do to make money and how I do it is very much a reflection of who I am. This is both blessing and a curse. For me, the personal and professional aspects of my life are very much inter-related and this is a fundamental fact that must be acknowledged.

For years, I have had a vague set of personal and professional objectives, but they lacked the clear structure that I believe I need to achieve true success. I recently came across a format for documenting these objectives that I think makes a lot of sense. The format comes from a book by William H. Davidson, called "The Three Components of Spiritual Reality: Physical, Spiritual and Divine." The book addresses three fundamental realms that we all interact with on a daily basis and suggests that success in any of these realms requires careful attention to both the realm itself and the intersection points with the others. This format for documenting objectives reflects these three realms.

I am in the process of documenting my objectives according to this format and I will share some of them in future posts. I would love to hear from you about how you document your objectives and how this format might be helpful to you.

Recent advances in quantum physics have revealed that the physical world is not as solid and predictable as we previously thought. At the subatomic level, matter and energy are governed by the principles of uncertainty, probability, and entanglement. These principles allow for the possibility of non-local effects, which means that events in one place can influence events in another place without any apparent cause or mechanism.

Research at the Princeton Engineering Anomalies Research (PEAR) laboratory has demonstrated that human consciousness can influence quantum events through intention. In their Global Consciousness Project, they measured the correlation between collective human intention and the behavior of random number generators during major world events. Their findings showed that human consciousness can affect the physical world through quantum-level interactions.

The Neuroscience of Intention:
Neuroscience has also provided insights into how intention works in the brain. Studies using functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) have identified several brain regions that are involved in setting and achieving goals, such as the prefrontal cortex, the anterior cingulate cortex, and the default mode network.

These regions work together to create a mental representation of our desired outcome, evaluate our current situation, and guide our actions accordingly. They also respond to feedback from our environment and adjust our behavior as needed.

for 2016? The year is still young enough for you to set some serious intentions. The question is, are you being realistic about your capabilities and the time you have available? Too often, we bite off more than we can chew, which ends up being demotivating and affecting our confidence.

The ancient Celts understood the concept of sacred geometries, and a number that appears repeatedly in their calculations is 40. For instance, they believed the human body contained 40 sacred points. What's more, the number 40 occurs frequently in various religious traditions, including Christianity, Judaism, and Hinduism.

Could the number 40 be significant in your life too? Consider how the concept of 40 days and 40 nights could apply to your personal and spiritual growth. The number appears throughout the Bible, from the 40 years the Israelites spent in the wilderness to Jesus' 40-day fast in the desert. While you might not be called to such extreme measures, the principle of focused spiritual practice over a period of 40 days could be transformative.

In our modern world, where constant connectivity and information overload are the norm, the idea of 40 days of focused spiritual practice might seem daunting. However, research in neuroscience and psychology increasingly supports the effectiveness of sustained spiritual practices in reducing stress, increasing emotional resilience, and promoting overall well-being.

The key is to approach this challenge with both realistic expectations and deep spiritual intention. Consider what practices you might incorporate: meditation, prayer, sacred geometrical visualization, or contemplative walks in nature. The choice depends on your personal spiritual path and what resonates with you.

For those new to spiritual practice, starting with just 15 minutes a day might be more manageable. The important thing is consistency and intention. As you progress, you might find yourself naturally drawn to longer periods of practice as your spiritual sensitivity increases.

Remember, the goal isn't merely to complete 40 days of practice, but to cultivate a deeper connection with your spiritual nature and the divine. This connection can provide comfort, guidance, and a profound sense of purpose that extends far beyond the initial 40-day period.

As you consider this challenge, reflect on what you hope to achieve through this spiritual journey. Increased inner peace? Greater spiritual awareness? A stronger connection to your religious tradition? Having clear intentions will help guide your practice and measure your progress.

The 40-day challenge isn't about forcing yourself

I know that seems like a low number, but I’ve noticed that I don’t often just sit and read. If I’m being honest, when I sit with my phone or tablet, I’m often checking social media. That’s a habit I want to break. Reading more and focusing on what I read is a good way to do that. 3. Track my writing progress in a bullet journal. I know some of my writing colleagues use bullet journals to track their progress on various projects and I’m intrigued by the process. I’ve noticed when I’m writing, I often just write without tracking my progress or keeping a daily count. By using a bullet journal to record my progress, I’m hoping to be more focused when I sit down to write. 4. Take more photographs on my phone. This is a personal goal that I think will benefit my writing. My phone is always with me and I’m often in interesting places. I used to think of my life in terms of years. My whole existence was marked with how many years I had been here. From the moment I was born my life was measured in years. After I graduated from high school, my years of school were added up to twelve. My whole life was measured in years.
In fact the very first time I was presented with the Trinity I remember thinking the Father God was the most ancient of all beings.
I used to think my life in terms of years until I was introduced to prayer journals.
Prayer journals are a collection of prayer records - thoughts, responses, and dates of those prayers.
The first journal I ever had was given to me by a youth pastor. The cover had "Prayer Journal" written across it with a picture of a sunrise. In it were the dates of the year and the space to record what I had prayed about.
I had been keeping a diary since I was a little girl, but this was the first time I had ever been presented with a structured way of recording prayer.
The journal helped me to be more intentional about my prayer life. It forced me to think about what I was praying for and why. It also helped me to see patterns in my prayer life.
Prayer journals are a valuable tool for spiritual growth and development. They can help us to be more intentional about our prayer life, see patterns in our prayer life, and document our spiritual journey.

in life? My goal is to be a clinical psychologist, more specifically a child clinical psychologist. I have always been very interested in how the mind works and how people think. I used to be very interested in what makes us different from other animals and the development of humanity but after I took AP Psychology and was exposed to new perspectives in Psychology I became more interested in the development of human beings and how we become who we are today. The class opened me up to so many new perspectives, especially developmental psychology, which studies how human beings develop from birth to death. The concept of nature versus nurture was what peaked my interest and led me to the path of psychology. Nature versus Nurture studies which has a stronger influence on who we become: genetic composition or life experiences. This theory led me to question how our development from birth to death is affected by our experiences and what we are exposed to growing up, how our brain develops and how our experiences and environment affect that development. Through my research of this topic, I found the field of study known as developmental psychopathology, which studies human development across the lifespan from an integrated bio- psychosocial perspective. The study of psychopathology includes both normal and abnormal developmental processes. The study of abnormal development can help to better understand normal development and vice versa. I find this field of study very interesting and feel very strongly that I would like to pursue a career in this field.

I plan on attending Boston College in the fall of 2019 and majoring in psychology. Boston College offers a Master of Social Work (MSW) program in conjunction with their PhD program in Clinical Psychology. The program is called Clinical Social Work and integrates both clinical and research training. The program is five years long and focuses on empirically supported interventions to address child and adolescent mental health concerns. The program uses a bio-psycho-social framework to examine child psychopathology and development, which is exactly what I am interested in. I believe that this program will help me to achieve my goal of becoming a child clinical psychologist. I hope that I will be able to help children and their families develop into the best versions of themselves.

In addition to my academic and career goals, I also have some personal life goals that I would like to achieve in the near future. First, I would like to develop better organizational skills to help me better balance my academic, social, and personal life. I believe this will help me to achieve my other personal goals. Second, I would like to become more spiritually connected, through prayer, meditation and

for this year? I had the opportunity to review this question with my team at the beginning of January and it was interesting to see how many people had specific measurable business goals. I was also pleased to see that we have a good balance between personal and professional goals for the year.

In our case, most of our personal goals were around health and fitness. Most of the team has a specific exercise plan. Some people were focusing on what they were going to cut out of their diet, while others were focusing on what they were going to add to their diet. I have to say, though, that the most specific and measurable goal was for someone who is training to run in the Boston Marathon.

I have a hard time with measurable goals related to health. Last year I said I was going to exercise 2-3 times per week. I did this for about 3 months. While that isn't too bad, it doesn't help me maintain my current weight. The reality is I have to do at least 5 days a week to maintain my current weight. The problem with that is that I don't particularly like working out for extended periods of time. I am not a naturally active person, so I have to force myself to do this.

This year I have a different approach to my health goals. I am focusing on what I need to add to my diet. I know what I need to cut out, but I have a hard time implementing it and maintaining it. The reality is that I have more control over what I add to my diet than what I cut out.

I have been reading a lot about bio-hacks recently. Some of it is too extreme for me to do on a regular basis. However, I am focusing on 4 specific things to add to my daily routine:

* 16 oz. water in the morning
* Pro-biomix (or any other pro-biotic)
* 2-3 scoops of collagen
* 2 tbsp of extra virgin coconut oil

I have been doing this for about 3 weeks now, and it is easy to do. In fact, my water intake has been better overall since I started this morning routine. I can't say for sure that it is helping me maintain my weight, but it isn't hurting either.

What are your health and fitness goals for 2019?

For instance:

1. I would like to work on my writing and my blog
2. I would like to do more exercise
3. I would like to read more
4. I would like to do more volunteer work
5. I would like to plan and complete some home improvement projects

How do I balance all of these things? How do I prioritize these activities?

I was listening to a morning meditation this morning and this question came up - what do you want to cultivate? What do you want to let go of?

The answer to that question was clear for me - I want to let go of being so focused on achieving and being productive. I need to cultivate more stillness and presence in my life.

In order to do that, I need to make conscious decisions about what I want to focus on and what I need to let go of.

For me, this year I have been trying to do too many things and none of them well. I have been trying to do too much and not being fully present in the moment.

In 2017, I want to focus on being more present and mindful in my daily activities. I want to let go of the need to always be doing something and just be in the moment.

I know that this will be a challenge, but I am committed to making this change. I hope that you will join me on this journey.

Her theoretical work on prayer encompasses what we already know: prayer generates energy frequencies that can be measured. We also know that sustained prayer generates more intense energy frequencies that can be measured. What is fascinating about her work is her observation that focused prayer generates measurable frequency patterns that vary in intensity according to the type of prayer. Contemplative prayer (or centering prayer) generates consistent patterns that are low intensity but deep and penetrating. Intercessory prayer generates patterns that are of higher frequency and intensity.
